notes: Original fill pack for categories undercovered by OpenTDB.
Designed for reviewable MVP volume rather than final tournament polish.

questions.clue: This Renaissance artist painted the Mona Lisa and designed flying-machine sketches.
This Spanish artist painted Guernica after the bombing of a Basque town.
This Dutch painter cut off part of his ear and painted Sunflowers.
This Mexican painter often used self-portraits, monkeys, and thorn imagery.
This French sculptor created The Thinker and The Burghers of Calais.
This Italian sculptor carved David before he painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling.
This American painter is linked with drip painting and abstract expressionism.
This painter of American Gothic often showed stern Midwestern subjects.
This Leonardo portrait is famous for an ambiguous smile.
This Munch painting shows a figure clutching its face beneath a blood-red sky.
This Botticelli painting shows Venus arriving on a shell.
This Seurat painting places leisure seekers on an island in pointillist dots.
questions.answer: A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte

questions.clue: This Vermeer painting is nicknamed for the jewelry worn by its turbaned subject.
aliases: The Girl with a Pearl Earring

questions.clue: This Diego Velazquez painting centers on the young Infanta Margarita and her attendants.
This large Delacroix canvas personifies revolution with a bare-breasted woman carrying the French tricolor.
Monet, Renoir, and Degas are strongly associated with this light-focused French movement.
Dalí and Magritte worked in this dreamlike movement shaped by the unconscious.
Picasso and Braque helped develop this movement of fractured planes and multiple viewpoints.
This post-World War I movement mocked reason and embraced absurdity.
This early-20th-century Italian movement celebrated speed, machines, and modern violence.
This ancient Greek building on the Acropolis was dedicated to Athena.
This Paris tower was built for the 1889 Exposition Universelle.
Frank Lloyd Wright designed this Pennsylvania house built over a waterfall.
This architect designed the Guggenheim Museum in New York and Fallingwater.
This Catalan architect designed Barcelona’s still-unfinished Sagrada Familia.
This Paris museum houses the Mona Lisa and the Venus de Milo.
This New York museum is commonly abbreviated MoMA.
This London museum displays the Rosetta Stone and the Elgin Marbles.
The Prado, known for Velazquez and Goya, is in this European capital.
This photographer captured Migrant Mother during the Great Depression.
This landscape photographer helped found Group f/64 and photographed Yosemite.
This French photographer is linked with the phrase the decisive moment.
This translucent Chinese ceramic became prized in Europe and is often called fine china.
This German school connected craft, modern design, and architecture from 1919 to 1933.
This late-19th-century style used whiplash curves, florals, and decorative unity.
This colossal figure in New York Harbor was a gift from France.
This Maya Lin-designed memorial in Washington lists names on polished black granite.
This Christ statue overlooks Rio de Janeiro from Mount Corcovado.
This technique uses small dots of color that blend in the viewer’s eye.
This wall-painting method applies pigment to wet plaster.
This dramatic contrast of light and dark is associated with Caravaggio.
This printmaking process uses acid to bite lines into a metal plate.
This technique creates an image from small pieces of colored stone, glass, or tile.
This powerful Florence family patronized Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Renaissance humanists.
This term describes artists who worked outside official academic traditions, often self-taught.
This group included Monet and Renoir after breaking from the official Paris Salon.
This American critic promoted abstract expressionism and championed flatness in modern painting.
This Peggy built a major modern art collection and opened Art of This Century in New York.
This Russian avant-garde movement included Malevich’s Black Square and emphasized pure geometric forms.
This Greek goddess of wisdom was born from Zeus’s head.
This hero killed Medusa and rescued Andromeda.
This musician looked back and lost Eurydice on the way out of the underworld.
This Titan was punished by having an eagle eat his liver.
This daughter of Minos helped Theseus escape the Labyrinth with a thread.
This mortal hunter saw Artemis bathing and was changed into a stag.
This Norse trickster is the father of Fenrir and Hel.
This Roman god of war gives his name to the month of March.
This Norse god traded an eye for wisdom at Mimir’s well.
This hammer-wielding Norse god rides in a chariot pulled by goats.
This Norse world tree connects the Nine Worlds.
This Roman two-faced god is linked with doorways, beginnings, and January.
This Egyptian jackal-headed god is associated with mummification and the dead.
This Egyptian goddess, wife of Osiris, reassembled him after Seth’s attack.
This Hindu remover of obstacles is depicted with an elephant head.
This feathered serpent deity appears in Aztec tradition.
In Japanese myth, this sun goddess emerges from a cave after being lured by a mirror and dance.
This first book of the Bible begins with creation.
This young shepherd defeated Goliath.
This prophet was swallowed by a great fish after fleeing toward Tarshish.
This apostle is associated with denial before the rooster crowed.
This Babylonian king dreams of a statue interpreted by Daniel.
This short New Testament letter asks Philemon to receive an escaped slave as a brother.
This religion’s Five Pillars include prayer, fasting, almsgiving, pilgrimage, and confession of faith.
This religion traces its Four Noble Truths to Siddhartha Gautama.
This Sikh house of worship serves a communal meal called langar.
This Jewish holiday commemorates the Exodus and includes a seder.
This Shinto shrine gate marks the transition into sacred space.
This Islamic holy book is traditionally understood as revealed to Muhammad.
This Sanskrit word can mean duty, law, or cosmic order in Indian religions.
This collection of rabbinic discussions includes the Mishnah and Gemara.
These ancient Indian texts include the Rigveda and Sama Veda.
This student of Socrates wrote the Republic.
This philosopher taught Alexander the Great and wrote the Nicomachean Ethics.
This French thinker began by doubting everything and wrote “I think, therefore I am.”
This German philosopher wrote the Critique of Pure Reason.
This utilitarian thinker wrote On Liberty and The Subjection of Women.
This Danish philosopher wrote Fear and Trembling under the pseudonym Johannes de Silentio.
This Hellenistic school taught apatheia and is associated with Epictetus and Marcus Aurelius.
This school founded by Epicurus argued that pleasure and freedom from disturbance are central goods.
This modern movement associated with Sartre emphasizes freedom, choice, and responsibility.
This school from Charles Sanders Peirce and William James measures ideas by practical consequences.
This ethical theory judges actions by their consequences for overall happiness.
This Hobbes book imagines life without government as solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short.
This Rawls concept asks people to design society without knowing their own place in it.
This Machiavelli treatise advises rulers on power and reputation.
This nine-branched candelabrum is used during Hanukkah.
This Hindu festival of lights celebrates themes of victory and renewal.
This Christian period of fasting and reflection precedes Easter.
This adjective means happening every two years.
This verb means to make amends or repair harm.
This noun means a short, witty saying or observation.
This adjective means noisy, energetic, and hard to control.
This adjective describes something that seems reasonable or believable.
This verb means to lessen the force or severity of something.
This adjective means harmful in a gradual or subtle way.
This noun means a remedy for all ills or difficulties.
This word for a school comes from Greek for leisure.
This word for a disaster comes from Italian words meaning bad star.
This word for salary comes from Latin salarium, linked with salt.
This word for a clue or hint comes from the thread Ariadne gave Theseus.
This word for a map book comes from a Titan forced to hold the sky.
This word for a tiny amount comes from Latin scrupulus, a small sharp stone.
This homophone of “pair” is a fruit often found in lunchboxes.
This homophone of “knight” is the opposite of day.
This homophone of “sole” can mean a person’s spiritual essence.
This homophone of “cite” is the ability to see.
This homophone of “principal” means a rule or belief.
This homophone of “stationary” means paper for writing letters.
Rearrange CARE to name a unit of land area.
Rearrange TEA to name something you consume.
Rearrange LISTEN to name what you should do while someone speaks.
Rearrange ELBOW to mean under or beneath.
Rearrange DORMITORY into a phrase meaning a messy room.
Rearrange ASTRONOMER into a phrase praising the moon.
Rearrange CONVERSATION into a phrase about speaking.
Breakfast cereal mascot Tony + award for Broadway theater.
Jane Austen novel + Steinbeck novella about George and Lennie.
questions.answer: Pride and Prejudice and Of Mice and Men
aliases: Pride and Prejudice and Of Mice and Men

questions.clue: Beatles album road + Sherlock Holmes address.
Shakespeare tragedy prince + small pork dish served at breakfast.
Disney snowman + Newton unit of force.
Greek hero of the Trojan War + tendon at the back of the ankle.
In internet slang, these three letters mean “laughing out loud.”
This two-letter postal abbreviation stands for California.
In publishing, this abbreviation means “for example.”
This abbreviation means “that is” and comes from Latin id est.
This acronym names the space telescope launched in 1990 and named for an astronomer.
This Spanish word means hello.
This French phrase means thank you very much.
This Italian phrase means sweet doing nothing.
This German loanword means pleasure at another’s misfortune.
This French phrase literally meaning already seen names a familiar feeling.
This Latin phrase means after the fact and often describes retroactive reasoning.
This part of speech names a person, place, thing, or idea.
This punctuation mark joins two independent clauses when no conjunction is used.
This verb mood is used for wishes or hypotheticals, as in “if I were.”
This word refers back to a noun, as “she” refers to Maria.
This term names a verb form functioning as a noun, such as “swimming” in “Swimming is fun.”
This misplaced modifier appears to attach to the wrong word in a sentence.
A rhyming phrase for a feline wearing a topper.
A rhyming phrase for a speedy vegetable.
Change one letter in COLD to get a precious metal.
Change one letter in SAND to get the body part that grips a pencil.
A rhyming phrase for a cautious legal document.
This idiom means to reveal a secret: let the cat out of the what?
This idiom means an obvious problem no one wants to discuss: the elephant in the what?
This idiom means to stop working for the day: call it a what?
This idiom means something is very easy: a piece of what?
This idiom means to make a small problem worse: add fuel to the what?
This idiom means to pursue the wrong course: bark up the wrong what?
This word means deliberately ambiguous or evasive, especially in language.
Opposite of north.
Opposite of left.
This word means a three-sided polygon.
This adjective means stubbornly refusing to change an opinion.
This word means the act of explaining or translating meaning.
This figure of speech describes one thing as another.
This word for a false name comes from Greek roots meaning false name.
This word for a word’s origin comes from Greek roots meaning true sense.
This homophone of “altar” means to change.
This homophone of “cereal” means arranged in a series.
Rearrange THE EYES into a phrase meaning “they observe.”
Rearrange ELEVEN PLUS TWO into an equivalent number phrase.
First U.S. president + Irving Berlin song about the holidays.
This acronym names the international organization founded after World War II to promote peace.
This acronym names the U.S. agency created in 1958 for space exploration.
This Latin phrase meaning “seize the day” is associated with Horace.
This Japanese word describes finding beauty in imperfection and transience.
This punctuation mark can indicate omitted letters, as in “don’t.”
This rhetorical device repeats initial consonant sounds in nearby words.
A rhyming phrase for a tiny violin.
A rhyming phrase for a sleepy royal ruler.
In this idiom, delaying action is kicking the can farther down this.
This idiom means a final tolerated problem: the last straw that broke the what?
One more than none.
Poet’s before.
Suffix meaning “one who.”
Self, in psychology.
Letter-shaped valley carved by glaciers.
This hospital department handles acute emergencies.
This quality means being able to read and write.
This word means an exaggerated love of one’s country.
This month name comes from the Roman god Janus.
This homophone of “discreet” means separate or distinct.
This adjective means using few words, terse, or concise.
Classic board game with Mr. Boddy + weather hint that makes you carry an umbrella.
This abbreviation means “manuscript” in publishing and library catalogs.
This idiom means to reveal your true intention: show your what?
